prompt_md: What does the `override` specifier do when placed on a derived class function?

options.a: It forces the base class to declare the function `virtual` automatically.
options.b: It asks the compiler to verify that the function actually overrides a virtual function in a base class, and reports an error if it does not.
options.c: It prevents any further derived class from overriding the function.
options.d: It changes the function's linkage to make it callable from C.

explanation_md: `override` is a compile-time check â€” it tells the compiler to verify the signature matches a virtual function in a base class. Option (c) describes `final`, not `override`.

answer: Compile error: 'render' does not override any base class virtual function
explanation_md: `Base::render` is `const` but `Derived::render` is not. Their signatures differ, so `Derived::render` is not an override of `Base::render`. With `override` specified, the compiler catches this mismatch and reports an error. Without `override` it would silently compile as an unrelated function.

options.a: `BigCircle::draw` is missing `override` so it shadows `Circle::draw` as a new non-virtual function, meaning `s->draw()` calls `Circle::draw`.
options.b: `BigCircle::draw` is missing `override` but that is fine; `s->draw()` should still dispatch to `BigCircle::draw`.
options.c: The virtual table only supports two levels of overriding.

explanation_md: The correct answer is (d) â€” the program actually prints `BigCircle`, not `Shape`. Once `draw` is declared `virtual` in `Shape`, all overrides in the hierarchy (including `BigCircle::draw`) participate in virtual dispatch even without explicit `virtual` or `override`. Option (a) is wrong: missing `override` does not prevent the override from working â€” it just removes the compile-time safety check.

prompt_md: What does marking a class `final` do?

options.a: Prevents any virtual functions in the class from being overridden by derived classes.
options.b: Prevents any other class from inheriting from this class.
options.c: Makes all member functions of the class non-virtual.
options.d: Prevents the class from having a destructor.

explanation_md: `final` on a class prevents further inheritance â€” any attempt to derive from it is a compile error. It does not change the virtual-ness of existing functions (option a), does not affect whether functions are virtual (option c), and has nothing to do with destructors (option d).
